methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

path: requestDeposit: user transfers underlying tokens to vault
queuedDeposit[user][depositEpoch] incremented
depositEpochState[depositEpoch].assetsDeposited incremented
fulfillDeposits: manager computes NAV, mints shares proportionally to totalSupply
depositEpoch incremented, unfulfilled remainder rolled to next epoch
executeDeposit: user's pro-rata share of fulfilled deposits calculated
_mintVirtual: shares credited to user
userBasis[user] updated with cost basis
unfulfilled remainder rolled to epoch+1
transformations: assets -> pro-rata fulfilled assets via mulDiv(queuedAmount, assetsFulfilled, assetsDeposited)
fulfilled assets -> shares via mulDiv(userAssets, sharesReceived, assetsFulfilled)
data_flows.trust_changes: User trusts manager to fulfill honestly; manager controls NAV input and fulfillment amounts; accountant is trusted for NAV computation
validation_points: Solidity overflow checks prevent assetsToFulfill > assetsDeposited
exposure_risks: Manager sees all pending deposits and can front-run or selectively fulfill
manipulation_risks: Manager can manipulate NAV via managerInput to accountant, inflating/deflating share price
Fee-on-transfer tokens would cause assetsDeposited to overstate actual vault balance
Double rounding in executeDeposit (two consecutive mulDiv) systematically shorts users on shares

path: requestWithdrawal: compute proportional basis from userBasis
_burnVirtual: decrement balanceOf[user] without touching totalSupply
withdrawalEpochState[withdrawalEpoch].sharesWithdrawn incremented
fulfillWithdrawals: manager computes NAV, calculates assets per share
withdrawalEpoch incremented, unfulfilled shares rolled to next epoch
reservedWithdrawalAssets incremented
executeWithdrawal: pro-rata assets and basis calculated
performance fee on profit deducted
assets transferred to user and fee to feeWallet
data_flows.trust_changes: User trusts manager for NAV accuracy; manager controls maxAssetsReceived cap; accountant trusted for NAV
exposure_risks: reservedWithdrawalAssets decremented before external transfer (potential reentrancy window)
Rollover to epoch+1 before external call allows re-entrant execution of epoch+1 if already fulfilled
manipulation_risks: Manager can manipulate NAV to underprice shares during withdrawal
Performance fee bypass if user acquires shares without basis tracking (unlikely due to _transferBasis)
Manager can cancel any user's withdrawal unilaterally

path: Decode managerInput into prices, pool info, and token IDs
Validate pools hash against stored vaultPools[vault]
For each pool: validate TWAP deviation from manager poolPrice
Fetch accumulated premiums and position balances from PanopticPool
Calculate liquidity amounts for each leg at manager-provided price
Add/subtract based on long/short, add exercised amounts
Add wallet balances and collateral token positions
Convert non-underlying token exposures using TWAP oracles
Clamp per-pool exposure to zero minimum
Sum across pools, add underlying balance if not already counted
transformations: Manager tick prices -> sqrtRatio for liquidity computation
Premium: token0 = short - long (rightSlot), token1 = long - short (leftSlot) [ASYMMETRIC]
sinks: nav return value used in fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals
data_flows.trust_changes: Manager prices validated against TWAP within maxPriceDeviation; pool configuration trusted via hash; TWAP oracle trusted for staleness check
validation_points: abs(managerPrice - TWAP) <= maxPriceDeviation for pool and token oracles
exposure_risks: Manager controls tick prices used for liquidity valuation within deviation band
maxPriceDeviation window allows manager to systematically bias NAV
TWAP can be manipulated with sufficient capital
manipulation_risks: Manager can pick tick prices at the extreme of the deviation band to inflate/deflate NAV
Per-pool clamping to 0 overstates NAV when some pools are underwater
Token1 premium sign appears inverted, causing incorrect NAV for positions with premium

path: _transferBasis: compute proportional basis from fromBalance
Deduct basisToTransfer from userBasis[from]
Add basisToTransfer to userBasis[to]
ERC20Minimal.transfer/transferFrom: move shares
data_flows.trust_changes: No external trust change; internal accounting only
validation_points: fromBalance == 0 returns early (prevents division by zero)
manipulation_risks: If fromBalance < amount, _transferBasis computes inflated basisToTransfer (more than total basis) before super.transfer reverts - but since it reverts, no issue
Basis is rounded down on transfer, so splitting shares across accounts slowly erodes aggregate basis, reducing performance fees

path: Manager calls manage(target, data, value)
functionCallWithValue executes arbitrary call from vault
Return data passed back to manager
sinks: Any external contract, vault state via self-calls
data_flows.trust_changes: Full trust in manager; can execute any action as the vault
exposure_risks: Manager can transfer all vault assets including reservedWithdrawalAssets
Manager can approve arbitrary spenders for vault tokens
Manager can call vault's own functions bypassing normal restrictions
manipulation_risks: Manager can drain all assets rendering withdrawals insolvent
Manager can manipulate vault token balances to affect NAV
Manager can call setManager/setAccountant if owner == vault (self-owned)

core_rules: Deposits and withdrawals are queued and fulfilled asynchronously by the manager
Share pricing is based on NAV computed by the accountant with manager-provided auxiliary data
Partially fulfilled epochs roll unfulfilled amounts to the next epoch
Performance fees are charged only on profitable withdrawals relative to cost basis
Cost basis tracks proportionally through share transfers
Phantom shares (1M totalSupply at construction) provide inflation attack protection
reservedWithdrawalAssets are segregated from manager-deployable capital
economic_incentives: Performance fee incentivizes vault to generate returns for depositors
Manager has discretion over fulfillment timing and amounts - misaligned with user interests
Users cannot force withdrawal - must wait for manager fulfillment
No management fee, only performance fee on profits
governance_mechanisms: Owner sets manager, accountant, and fee wallet with no timelock
Accountant owner controls pool configurations with lock mechanism
Manager has unilateral power to cancel any user's deposit or withdrawal
No mechanism for users to vote or influence vault operations

edge_cases.scenario: Per-pool NAV clamping to zero instead of aggregate clamping
edge_cases.expected_behavior: NAV should reflect true net value: sum all pool exposures then clamp to zero if negative
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Each pool's exposure is individually clamped to zero via Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0), then summed. If Pool A = +100 and Pool B = -200, NAV = 100 instead of 0
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Manager can structure positions to have underwater pools (ignored in NAV) alongside profitable pools, overstating NAV. This inflates share prices, allowing manager or colluding depositors to extract value from existing shareholders during withdrawal fulfillment

edge_cases.scenario: Token1 premium sign inversion in PanopticVaultAccountant
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Net premium for token1 should be shortPremium.leftSlot() - longPremium.leftSlot() (same sign convention as token0)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Code computes poolExposure1 = longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot(), which inverts the sign relative to token0's treatment
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Incorrect premium accounting leads to systematic NAV miscalculation. If the vault is net short on token1, premium earned is subtracted from NAV instead of added, understating NAV and allowing manager to buy shares cheaply during deposits or extract value during withdrawals

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Vault should account for actual tokens received after transfer fees
edge_cases.actual_behavior: requestDeposit records `assets` parameter directly into queuedDeposit and depositEpochState, but vault receives fewer tokens due to transfer fee. This creates a shortfall.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If underlying is fee-on-transfer, the vault has fewer tokens than accounted for. Last withdrawers are unable to withdraw their full entitlement, as the vault's actual balance is less than the recorded deposits. Effectively, early withdrawers extract value at the expense of later ones.

edge_cases.scenario: Double rounding in executeDeposit pro-rata share calculation
edge_cases.expected_behavior: User receives shares proportional to their deposit
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Two consecutive mulDiv operations (assets fulfilled, then shares received) each round down independently. This compounds rounding error, causing users to receive fewer shares than a single-step calculation would yield
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Systematic extraction of dust amounts from depositors. Over many users and epochs, the unclaimed shares accumulate in the contract, permanently locked since no one can claim them

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Graceful handling when vault is underwater
edge_cases.actual_behavior: totalAssets calculation underflows, causing revert. Manager cannot fulfill any deposits, effectively locking deposited funds
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If vault takes losses, deposit fulfillment becomes impossible until NAV recovers. Users' deposited funds are stuck with no withdrawal path since they haven't received shares yet. Only cancelDeposit (manager-only) can return funds.

edge_cases.scenario: First depositor dilution from phantom 1M totalSupply + 1 offset
edge_cases.expected_behavior: First depositor receives shares at fair value
edge_cases.actual_behavior: With totalAssets = 1 (from +1 offset) and totalSupply = 1_000_000, the first depositor gets assetsToFulfill * 1_000_000 shares. Their proportional claim is assets / (assets + 1), losing approximately 1/assets of their deposit to the phantom shares permanently
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: For small initial deposits, the loss is material (e.g., 1% for 100-unit deposit). An attacker could front-run the first real deposit with a tiny deposit to set unfavorable share pricing. However, since the manager controls fulfillment, this is mitigated.

edge_cases.scenario: Manager provides maxPriceDeviation-edge tick prices to bias NAV
edge_cases.expected_behavior: NAV should reflect fair market value within the deviation tolerance
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Manager can consistently choose tick prices at the extreme of the allowed deviation band, systematically overstating or understating NAV by up to the full maxPriceDeviation in each direction
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Manager can inflate NAV during deposit fulfillment (diluting new depositors' shares) or deflate NAV during withdrawal fulfillment (underpaying withdrawers). The deviation band creates a consistent extraction opportunity.

edge_cases.scenario: Reentrancy in executeWithdrawal with multi-epoch rollover
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Each epoch's withdrawal is executed independently and correctly
edge_cases.actual_behavior: executeWithdrawal rolls unfulfilled shares to epoch+1 BEFORE external token transfers. If epoch+1 is already fulfilled, a reentrant call via token transfer callback could execute epoch+1 with the inflated queuedWithdrawal amount. The user's combined share of epoch+1 would include both original and rolled-over amounts.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Requires underlying token with callbacks AND epoch+1 already fulfilled. The user's pro-rata share increases at the expense of other withdrawers in epoch+1 who haven't executed yet. However, total distributed cannot exceed reservedWithdrawalAssets for that epoch.

edge_cases.scenario: fulfillDeposits or fulfillWithdrawals with zero amount
edge_cases.actual_behavior: fulfillDeposits(0, ...) creates epoch state with 0 assetsFulfilled and 0 sharesReceived, increments depositEpoch. executeDeposit for this epoch would compute sharesReceived = mulDiv(X, 0, 0) which reverts (division by zero). All deposits roll over but cannot be executed.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Manager (accidentally or maliciously) calling fulfillDeposits(0, ...) creates an unexecutable epoch. Users' deposits in that epoch are trapped until rollover through executeDeposit, which will revert due to division by zero on assetsFulfilled. Only the manager calling cancelDeposit from the NEXT epoch could help, but the user's deposit is no longer tracked in the current epoch.

edge_cases.scenario: executeDeposit called after epoch+1 is also fulfilled with zero assetsFulfilled
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Unfulfilled amounts keep rolling forward correctly
edge_cases.actual_behavior: If executeDeposit for epoch N rolls remainder to N+1, and epoch N+1 has assetsDeposited but was fulfilled with 0 assetsFulfilled, the user's rolled-over amount is now in a broken epoch where executeDeposit would revert with division by zero
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Chain of zero-fulfillment epochs permanently locks user deposits

edge_cases.scenario: requestWithdrawal with shares equal to total balance, followed by transfer of basis-bearing shares to same user
edge_cases.actual_behavior: After requestWithdrawal, userBasis[msg.sender] is reduced proportionally. If user then receives shares via transfer, they get new basis. On withdrawal execution, the old basis is used for performance fee calculation. The new shares have separate tracking since they're in the user's balance. No exploit since basis is correctly tracked.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No direct exploit, but complex interactions between pending withdrawals and new share acquisitions could confuse off-chain accounting

edge_cases.scenario: Collateral tokens counted per pool but underlying wallet balance counted only once
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Each asset source counted exactly once
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The skipToken0/skipToken1 mechanism prevents double-counting wallet balances, but collateral token positions (collateralToken0/collateralToken1) are counted for EVERY pool. If a vault has positions in multiple pools sharing the same underlying token, the collateral in each pool's CollateralTracker is correctly counted separately since they are different contracts. However, the wallet balance for the same token is only counted once.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No direct exploit - collateral trackers are separate contracts so their balances are independent

edge_cases.scenario: Manager calls manage() to transfer underlying tokens, draining reservedWithdrawalAssets
edge_cases.expected_behavior: reservedWithdrawalAssets should always be backed by actual token balance
edge_cases.actual_behavior: manage() can execute arbitrary calls including transferring the underlying token. There is no check that the vault retains at least reservedWithdrawalAssets worth of underlying tokens. Subsequent executeWithdrawal calls would fail due to insufficient balance.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Malicious or compromised manager can drain reserved withdrawal assets, making fulfilled withdrawals unexecutable. Users who burned shares for withdrawal requests lose their shares permanently.

assumption_violations: Assumes underlying token is standard ERC20 with no transfer fees, rebasing, or callback hooks
Assumes manager acts honestly in NAV computation, fulfillment amounts, and capital deployment
Assumes accountant correctly computes NAV and is not compromised
Assumes TWAP oracles cannot be manipulated beyond maxPriceDeviation during fulfillment
Assumes per-pool NAV clamping is equivalent to aggregate NAV clamping (it is not)
Assumes premium sign convention is intentionally asymmetric for token0 vs token1
Assumes vault will never be underwater (NAV < deposits + reserved) or fulfillment becomes impossible
Assumes all positions are reported in tokenIds array (validated by numberOfLegs check)
game_theory_risks: Manager can time fulfillments to maximize/minimize NAV for personal benefit
Manager can selectively cancel deposits/withdrawals to disadvantage specific users
Information asymmetry: manager sees all pending deposits/withdrawals and NAV in real-time, users do not
Users have no recourse if manager delays fulfillment indefinitely
Basis erosion through repeated transfers allows gradual performance fee reduction via dust rounding
If multiple users have pending withdrawals, early executors get exact pro-rata share; late executors may find reservedWithdrawalAssets slightly short due to rounding
integration_risks: PanopticPool.getAccumulatedFeesAndPositionsData must return correct premium data - any bug propagates to NAV
CollateralTracker.previewRedeem must accurately reflect redeemable value
TWAP oracle manipulation via flash loans or multi-block MEV could bias NAV within deviation band
ERC20Minimal lacks SafeERC20 pattern - non-compliant tokens could silently fail transfers (mitigated by SafeTransferLib)
Panoptic v1.1 upgrade or migration could break accountant assumptions
IV3CompatibleOracle staleness is only checked against manager price, not against time-based freshness
calculation_errors: Token1 premium uses long - short instead of short - long, inverting the contribution to NAV relative to token0
Per-pool Math.max(exposure, 0) clamping overstates aggregate NAV when any pool is underwater

Double mulDiv in executeDeposit compounds rounding: first (queuedAmount * assetsFulfilled / assetsDeposited), then (result * sharesReceived / assetsFulfilled). Single-step mulDiv(queuedAmount, sharesReceived, assetsDeposited) would be more precise

The +1 in totalAssets = NAV + 1 - deposits - reserved creates asymmetric pricing: during deposits it deflates share price slightly (fewer shares per asset), during withdrawals it inflates share price slightly (fewer assets per share withdrawn). This systematically extracts value from users.
int256 cast of uint256 in performance fee calculation (int256(assetsToWithdraw) - int256(withdrawnBasis)) could overflow for values > type(int256).max, though practically unlikely

token_semantics.notes: Vault assumes standard ERC20 behavior. Fee-on-transfer tokens cause accounting mismatch between recorded deposits and actual balance. Rebasing tokens would similarly break NAV accounting. No received-amount validation on transfers.

ERC20Minimal-based vault shares. Virtual mint/burn used for async deposit/withdrawal. totalSupply initialized to 1_000_000 phantom shares as inflation protection. No permit support. Basis tracking on transfer/transferFrom.
Panoptic CollateralTracker uses share-based accounting. previewRedeem used to convert shares to underlying. Assumed to be standard ERC4626-like.

invariants.name: supply_conservation
invariants.notes: totalSupply should equal sum of all balanceOf PLUS shares in pending withdrawals (virtually burned from balanceOf but still in totalSupply until fulfillment). Constructor initializes totalSupply=1_000_000 with no corresponding balanceOf entries.

invariants.name: reserved_assets_backing
invariants.notes: reservedWithdrawalAssets should always be <= vault's underlying token balance. Violated if manager uses manage() to transfer underlying tokens out.

invariants.name: epoch_deposit_consistency
invariants.notes: Sum of all users' queued deposits in epoch e should equal depositEpochState[e].assetsDeposited. Rollovers maintain this because aggregate rollover is set in fulfillDeposits and per-user rollover in executeDeposit. Rounding may cause slight discrepancy.

invariants.name: epoch_withdrawal_consistency
invariants.notes: Sum of all users' queued withdrawal amounts in epoch e should equal withdrawalEpochState[e].sharesWithdrawn. Same rounding caveat as deposits.

invariants.name: basis_conservation
invariants.notes: Total basis in active balances plus basis in pending withdrawals should be conserved. Rounding in _transferBasis and proportional calculations causes slow erosion.
